biomarkers.name: Apolipoprotein B
biomarkers.physiological_role: Apolipoprotein B is the primary structural protein of LDL (low-density lipoprotein) particles. It serves as a ligand for LDL receptors and mediates the uptake of cholesterol by peripheral tissues. Elevated ApoB levels are associated with increased cardiovascular disease risk as it is the particle number indicator for atherogenic lipoproteins.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Strong epidemiological evidence; ApoB is considered a superior marker to LDL-C for cardiovascular risk prediction
Direct role in arterial plaque formation

dietary_interventions.intervention: Replace refined carbohydrates with whole grains

biomarker_interactions.description: ApoB is the protein component of LDL particles; they are highly correlated
VLDL remnants also contain ApoB
Inverse relationship in lipid metabolism

biomarkers.name: Thyroid-stimulating hormone
biomarkers.physiological_role: TSH is produced by the anterior pituitary gland and regulates thyroid hormone production (T3 and T4). It is the primary screening test for thyroid function and reflects the hypothalamic-pituitary-thyroid axis status. TSH influences metabolic rate, heart function, and cholesterol metabolism.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Elevated TSH with low T4 indicates primary hypothyroidism
Low TSH with elevated T3/T4 indicates hyperthyroidism
Both high and low TSH associated with increased CV risk

dietary_interventions.intervention: Reduce goitrogenic foods (soy, cruciferous) if iodine deficient

biomarker_interactions.description: TSH regulates T4 production; negative feedback loop
TSH stimulates T3 production

biomarkers.name: High-density lipoprotein cholesterol
biomarkers.physiological_role: HDL cholesterol is known as "good cholesterol" because it mediates reverse cholesterol transport, carrying excess cholesterol from peripheral tissues to the liver for excretion. HDL also has anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and endothelial protective effects.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low HDL is an independent cardiovascular risk factor
Low HDL is a component of metabolic syndrome diagnosis

dietary_interventions.intervention: Increase monounsaturated fat intake (olive oil, nuts)

biomarker_interactions.description: Inverse relationship; HDL helps remove cholesterol from arteries
Inverse relationship; high TG often with low HDL
ApoB/HDL ratio is used for risk assessment

biomarkers.name: Low-density lipoprotein cholesterol
biomarkers.physiological_role: LDL is the primary carrier of cholesterol to peripheral tissues and is central to atherosclerotic plaque formation. When LDL particles become oxidized or modified, they infiltrate arterial walls and contribute to plaque development.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Direct role in arterial plaque formation; LDL-C lowering reduces CV events
Strong dose-response relationship between LDL-C and CV risk

dietary_interventions.intervention: Replace refined carbs with whole grains

biomarker_interactions.description: LDL particles contain ApoB protein; directly correlated
Inverse relationship in cardiovascular risk
VLDL remnants contribute to small dense LDL

biomarkers.name: Triglycerides
biomarkers.physiological_role: Triglycerides are the main form of fat stored in adipose tissue and are transported in blood as VLDL particles. Elevated triglycerides contribute to arterial inflammation and are associated with increased cardiovascular risk, particularly when combined with low HDL.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Independent risk factor, especially with low HDL
Severe hypertriglyceridemia (>500mg/dL) increases pancreatitis risk
Elevated TG is a component of metabolic syndrome

dietary_interventions.intervention: Reduce added sugars and refined carbohydrates

biomarker_interactions.description: Inverse relationship; high TG typically with low HDL
VLDL metabolism affects LDL levels
Both elevated in insulin resistance

biomarkers.name: Total cholesterol
biomarkers.physiological_role: Total cholesterol encompasses all cholesterol carried in lipoproteins (LDL, HDL, VLDL). While it's a useful screening measure, the distribution among lipoprotein fractions and the total-to-HDL ratio provide more actionable information about cardiovascular risk.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Elevated total cholesterol increases CV risk, especially with high LDL

dietary_interventions.intervention: Reduce saturated and trans fat intake
Increase fruit, vegetable, and whole grain intake

biomarker_interactions.description: LDL-C contributes the largest portion to total cholesterol
HDL-C is a component of total cholesterol

biomarkers.name: High-sensitivity C-reactive protein
biomarkers.physiological_role: hsCRP is an acute-phase inflammatory marker produced by the liver in response to inflammation, infection, or tissue injury. It is used as a sensitive marker of systemic inflammation and is an independent predictor of cardiovascular events. The high-sensitivity assay detects lower levels suitable for cardiovascular risk assessment.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Elevated hsCRP is an independent predictor of MI, stroke, and CV death
Persistently elevated hsCRP indicates chronic inflammatory state
Rapidly elevated in acute bacterial and viral infections

biomarker_interactions.description: Both are inflammatory markers; WBC is less specific
Iron can promote inflammatory oxidation
IL-6 stimulates hepatic CRP production

biomarkers.name: Estradiol
biomarkers.physiological_role: Estradiol is the primary female sex hormone (estrogen) and is also present in males. In women, it regulates menstrual cycles, bone maintenance, and cardiovascular health. In men, it is important for bone health and sexual function. It is produced by ovaries, adipose tissue, and in men by aromatization of testosterone.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Optimal levels required for menstrual and reproductive function
Low E2 in postmenopausal women increases bone loss
Optimal E2 is protective; both deficiency and excess may increase risk

biomarker_interactions.description: E2/P4 ratio important for hormonal balance
Aromatase converts testosterone to E2
SHBG binds and regulates bioavailable E2

biomarkers.name: Progesterone
biomarkers.physiological_role: Progesterone is a steroid hormone essential for female reproductive health. It prepares the endometrium for pregnancy after ovulation and maintains pregnancy. In men, it serves as a precursor to other hormones and has roles in neuroprotection and myelin formation.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Inadequate progesterone in luteal phase linked to infertility
Progesterone fluctuations affect mood and physical symptoms

biomarker_interactions.description: E2/P4 ratio important for hormonal balance
LH stimulates progesterone production in ovaries

biomarkers.name: Insulin
biomarkers.physiological_role: Insulin is a peptide hormone produced by pancreatic beta cells that regulates blood glucose levels. It promotes cellular glucose uptake, glycogen synthesis, and inhibits gluconeogenesis. Insulin resistance is a hallmark of metabolic syndrome and type 2 diabetes.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Insulin resistance precedes and predicts T2DM development
Hyperinsulinemia is a component of metabolic syndrome
Insulin resistance associated with increased CV risk

biomarker_interactions.description: Insulin regulates blood glucose levels
Chronic hyperglycemia reflected in HbA1c
C-peptide is co-secreted with insulin

biomarkers.name: Dehydroepiandrosterone sulfate
biomarkers.physiological_role: DHEAS is the most abundant circulating steroid hormone and serves as a precursor to testosterone and estrogens. It has direct androgenic and estrogenic effects and is involved in immune function, bone health, and well-being. DHEAS levels decline with age.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low DHEAS indicates adrenal dysfunction
DHEAS declines significantly after age 30
DHEAS linked to bone mineral density in elderly

biomarker_interactions.description: DHEA is the precursor to testosterone
Both produced by adrenal glands; inverse relationship

biomarkers.name: Free testosterone
biomarkers.physiological_role: Free testosterone represents the unbound, biologically active fraction of testosterone circulating in blood. Total testosterone includes both free and protein-bound (to SHBG and albumin) fractions. Free T is important for assessing androgen status, particularly in older men and women with symptoms.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low free T with symptoms indicates androgen deficiency
Testosterone essential for maintaining muscle mass
Testosterone modulates sexual desire and function

biomarker_interactions.description: Free T is a fraction of total testosterone
SHBG binds testosterone, reducing free fraction
Albumin loosely binds testosterone

biomarkers.name: Calcium
biomarkers.physiological_role: Calcium is essential for bone structure, muscle contraction, nerve transmission, blood coagulation, and cellular signaling. About 99% of body calcium is in bone; the remainder circulates in blood. Serum calcium is tightly regulated by parathyroid hormone, vitamin D, and calcitonin.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Both low and high calcium affect bone metabolism
PTH regulates calcium homeostasis

biomarker_interactions.description: Vitamin D enhances calcium absorption
PTH regulates serum calcium levels
Calcium-phosphorus product important for bone health

biomarkers.name: Cortisol
biomarkers.physiological_role: Cortisol is the primary glucocorticoid produced by the adrenal cortex in response to stress and circadian rhythm. It regulates metabolism, immune function, blood pressure, and the sleep-wake cycle. Chronic elevation is associated with weight gain, insulin resistance, and cardiovascular risk.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Persistently elevated cortisol causes characteristic symptoms
Low cortisol causes fatigue, hypotension, and weakness
Prolonged cortisol elevation linked to weight gain and metabolic dysfunction

biomarker_interactions.description: Cortisol has anti-inflammatory effects but chronic elevation promotes inflammation
Cortisol increases gluconeogenesis and blood glucose
Inverse relationship in adrenal hormone balance

biomarkers.name: Sex-hormone binding globulin
biomarkers.physiological_role: SHBG is a glycoprotein that binds and transports testosterone, estradiol, and other steroid hormones in blood. It regulates the availability of free (biologically active) hormones. SHBG levels are influenced by hormones, insulin, liver function, and inflammatory state.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: SHBG affects bioavailable testosterone
Low SHBG common in PCOS with insulin resistance
Low SHBG associated with insulin resistance and obesity

biomarker_interactions.description: SHBG binds free testosterone, reducing bioavailable fraction
Reciprocal regulation with testosterone
Insulin suppresses SHBG production

biomarkers.name: Testosterone
biomarkers.physiological_role: Testosterone is the primary male sex hormone (androgen) produced mainly by the testes in men and ovaries in women, with adrenal contribution. It is essential for male reproductive development, muscle mass, bone density, red blood cell production, and well-being. Levels decline with age in men.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low testosterone causes symptoms of androgen deficiency
Testosterone helps maintain muscle mass and strength
Testosterone is important for bone health in men

biomarker_interactions.description: SHBG binds testosterone, regulating free fraction
LH stimulates testosterone production
DHEA is the precursor to testosterone

biomarkers.name: Vitamin D (25-hydroxyvitamin D)
biomarkers.physiological_role: Vitamin D is a fat-soluble vitamin that functions as a hormone, regulating calcium absorption, bone metabolism, immune function, and cell growth. It is synthesized in skin upon sun exposure or obtained from diet and supplements. 25-hydroxyvitamin D is the major circulating form and best reflects vitamin D status.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Vitamin D essential for calcium absorption and bone health
Vitamin D deficiency causes proximal muscle weakness
Vitamin D modulates both innate and adaptive immunity
Low vitamin D associated with depression in some studies

dietary_interventions.intervention: Vitamin D-rich foods (fatty fish, fortified foods)

biomarker_interactions.description: Vitamin D enhances intestinal calcium absorption
PTH increases when vitamin D is low
Vitamin D enhances phosphorus absorption

biomarkers.name: Red blood cell magnesium
biomarkers.physiological_role: RBC magnesium measures magnesium levels inside red blood cells, providing a better indicator of intracellular magnesium status than serum magnesium. Magnesium is essential for over 300 enzymatic reactions, including ATP production, muscle function, and nerve transmission.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low RBC Mg indicates cellular magnesium depletion
Magnesium deficiency causes neuromuscular irritability
Magnesium critical for cardiac electrical activity

biomarker_interactions.description: RBC Mg and serum Mg reflect different magnesium pools
Magnesium is required for potassium intracellular shift
Magnesium-calcium balance important for muscle function

biomarkers.name: Magnesium
biomarkers.physiological_role: Magnesium is a critical mineral involved in energy metabolism, protein synthesis, muscle and nerve function, blood glucose control, and blood pressure regulation. It serves as a cofactor for many enzymes and is important for ATP-dependent reactions.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low magnesium associated with elevated blood pressure
Magnesium involved in insulin action and glucose metabolism
Both low and high magnesium affect cardiovascular risk

dietary_interventions.intervention: Increase magnesium-rich foods (leafy greens, nuts, legumes)

biomarker_interactions.description: Both reflect magnesium status; RBC may be more sensitive
Magnesium required to maintain potassium intracellularly
Magnesium-calcium balance important for vascular tone

biomarkers.name: Glucose
biomarkers.physiological_role: Glucose is the primary energy source for cells and the only energy source for the brain under normal conditions. Blood glucose levels are tightly regulated by insulin and glucagon. Impaired fasting glucose indicates insulin resistance and is a prediabetic state.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Elevated fasting glucose is diagnostic of diabetes when >=126 mg/dL
Impaired fasting glucose (100-125 mg/dL) indicates prediabetes
Glucose <70 mg/dL causes neurological symptoms

biomarker_interactions.description: Insulin regulates glucose uptake into cells
HbA1c reflects average glucose over 2-3 months
Cortisol increases blood glucose

biomarkers.name: Hemoglobin A1c
biomarkers.physiological_role: HbA1c is hemoglobin with attached glucose (glycation) and reflects average blood glucose levels over the preceding 2-3 months (lifespan of red blood cells). It is used for diagnosing diabetes and prediabetes and for monitoring long-term glycemic control.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Higher HbA1c associated with increased risk of microvascular complications
Elevated HbA1c is an independent CV risk factor

biomarker_interactions.description: HbA1c reflects average blood glucose over 2-3 months
Both reflect glycemic status
Insulin resistance drives elevated HbA1c

biomarkers.name: White blood cell count
biomarkers.physiological_role: White blood cells are the immune system's effector cells, protecting against infections and foreign substances. Different WBC types (neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils) have distinct functions in innate and adaptive immunity.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Elevated WBC indicates infection; very low increases infection risk
Elevated WBC associated with cardiovascular disease and metabolic syndrome
Abnormal WBC counts may indicate leukemia or lymphoma

biomarker_interactions.description: Neutrophils are the largest WBC fraction
Lymphocytes are key adaptive immune cells
Both are inflammatory markers

biomarkers.name: Neutrophils
biomarkers.physiological_role: Neutrophils are the most abundant white blood cells and serve as the primary defense against bacterial infections. They are phagocytic cells that engulf and destroy pathogens. Elevated neutrophils (neutrophilia) indicates acute bacterial infection or inflammatory condition.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Neutrophilia is a hallmark of acute bacterial infection
Elevated neutrophils associated with chronic inflammatory states

biomarker_interactions.description: Neutrophils are a major component of total WBC
NLR (neutrophil/lymphocyte ratio) is a marker of inflammation

biomarkers.name: Basophils
biomarkers.physiological_role: Basophils are rare white blood cells that play roles in allergic reactions, asthma, and parasite infections. They release histamine and heparin and are involved in modulating immune responses.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Basophil activation in immediate hypersensitivity reactions
Basophilia may indicate chronic myeloid leukemia

biomarker_interactions.description: Basophils are a small fraction of total WBC
Both involved in allergic/parasitic responses

biomarkers.name: Eosinophils
biomarkers.physiological_role: Eosinophils are white blood cells involved in combating parasitic infections and in allergic/immune reactions. They release cytotoxic granule contents that damage parasites but can also cause tissue damage in allergic conditions.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Eosinophilia is characteristic of parasitic infections
Elevated eosinophils in allergic diseases and eosinophilic asthma
Persistently elevated eosinophils can cause organ damage

biomarker_interactions.description: Both involved in allergic/parasitic responses
Both indicate inflammatory/allergic processes

biomarkers.name: Lymphocytes
biomarkers.physiological_role: Lymphocytes are the key cells of adaptive immunity, including T cells (cellular immunity), B cells (humoral immunity), and NK cells (innate immunity). They are essential for specific recognition of pathogens and immunological memory.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low lymphocytes (lymphopenia) increases infection risk
CD4+ T cell count is key marker of HIV progression
Elevated lymphocytes may indicate CLL or lymphoma

biomarker_interactions.description: Lymphocytes are a major WBC fraction
NLR ratio is an inflammatory marker

biomarkers.name: Monocytes
biomarkers.physiological_role: Monocytes are large phagocytic leukocytes that differentiate into macrophages and dendritic cells in tissues. They are important for phagocytosis of pathogens, antigen presentation, and cytokine production in immune responses.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Monocytes drive chronic inflammatory responses and atherosclerosis
Monocyte dysfunction seen in various autoimmune conditions

biomarker_interactions.description: Monocytes produce inflammatory cytokines that stimulate CRP

biomarkers.name: Ferritin
biomarkers.physiological_role: Ferritin is the primary iron storage protein and reflects body iron stores. It is also an acute-phase reactant elevated in inflammation, infection, and liver disease. Ferritin is essential for diagnosing iron deficiency (low) and iron overload (high) conditions.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low ferritin with low hemoglobin indicates iron deficiency
Persistently elevated ferritin suggests iron overload
Ferritin elevated in anemia of chronic disease

dietary_interventions.intervention: Vitamin C to enhance iron absorption
Avoid excessive red meat (if iron overloaded)

biomarker_interactions.description: Ferritin stores iron; directly correlated with iron stores
Both reflect iron status
T IBC increases when iron is low
Ferritin is an acute phase reactant

biomarkers.name: Alanine aminotransferase
biomarkers.physiological_role: ALT is an enzyme found primarily in liver cells. It is released into blood when hepatocytes are damaged and is a sensitive marker of liver injury. ALT is used to screen for liver disease and monitor liver function during drug therapy.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Elevated ALT indicates hepatocyte injury
ALT often elevated in fatty liver disease

biomarker_interactions.description: AST/ALT ratio used to differentiate liver disease causes
GGT elevated in biliary disease and alcohol use

biomarkers.name: Aspartate aminotransferase
biomarkers.physiological_role: AST is an enzyme found in liver, heart, skeletal muscle, and red blood cells. It is released with tissue damage and is less liver-specific than ALT. AST/ALT ratio helps differentiate causes of liver injury.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Elevated AST with ALT helps characterize liver disease
AST elevated with strenuous exercise or muscle injury

biomarker_interactions.description: AST/ALT ratio used clinically; ratio >2 suggests alcoholic liver disease
Both elevated in muscle damage

biomarkers.name: Gamma-glutamyl transpeptidase
biomarkers.physiological_role: GGT is an enzyme involved in glutathione metabolism and amino acid transport. It is found in liver, biliary tract, and kidney. GGT is a sensitive marker of biliary obstruction, liver disease, and alcohol use. It is often elevated before other liver enzymes in liver disease.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: GGT highly sensitive to biliary obstruction
GGT elevated with chronic alcohol consumption
Elevated GGT associated with CV events even within normal range

biomarker_interactions.description: GGT often elevated with other liver enzymes
AST/ALT/GGT pattern helps identify liver disease cause

biomarkers.name: Albumin
biomarkers.physiological_role: Albumin is the most abundant plasma protein, produced by the liver. It maintains oncotic pressure, transports hormones, drugs, and nutrients, and serves as a marker of nutritional status and liver function.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low albumin indicates protein malnutrition
Low albumin reflects impaired liver synthetic function
Albumin decreases in inflammatory states (negative acute phase reactant)

biomarker_interactions.description: Both reflect nutritional status; prealbumin turns over faster
CRP elevated when albumin is low (negative acute phase reactant)

biomarkers.name: Creatine kinase
biomarkers.physiological_role: Creatine kinase is an enzyme found in skeletal muscle, heart, and brain. It catalyzes the transfer of phosphate from phosphocreatine to ADP, regenerating ATP. CK is released with muscle damage and is used to monitor athletic training and muscle injury.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: CK elevated after muscle injury, strenuous exercise
Very high CK indicates muscle breakdown
CK-MB isoform specific for cardiac muscle

biomarker_interactions.description: Both elevated with muscle damage
LDH elevated with tissue damage

biomarkers.name: Sodium
biomarkers.physiological_role: Sodium is the primary extracellular cation and is essential for maintaining fluid balance, blood pressure, and nerve/muscle function. Serum sodium is tightly regulated by the kidneys, aldosterone, and antidiuretic hormone.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low sodium causes neurological symptoms and can be life-threatening
High sodium causes dehydration and neurological symptoms
Elevated sodium indicates fluid loss

biomarker_interactions.description: Sodium-potassium pump maintains balance
Both are major extracellular electrolytes

biomarkers.name: Potassium
biomarkers.physiological_role: Potassium is the primary intracellular cation, essential for nerve conduction, muscle contraction, and maintaining cellular electrical potential. It is critical for cardiac function and is regulated by the kidneys, aldosterone, and acid-base status.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Both high and low potassium can cause fatal arrhythmias
Potassium imbalances affect muscle function

biomarker_interactions.description: Sodium-potassium pump maintains gradient
Magnesium required for potassium intracellular balance

biomarkers.name: Vitamin B12
biomarkers.physiological_role: Vitamin B12 is essential for DNA synthesis, red blood cell formation, and neurological function. It serves as a cofactor for methionine synthase and methylmalonyl-CoA mutase. Deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia and neurological damage.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: B12 deficiency causes megaloblastic anemia
B12 deficiency causes irreversible neurological damage
B12 required for methylation cycle

biomarker_interactions.description: Both involved in methylation cycle; B12 activates folate
B12 and folate normalize elevated homocysteine
MMA elevated specifically in B12 deficiency

biomarkers.name: Folate
biomarkers.physiological_role: Folate is essential for DNA synthesis, amino acid metabolism, and methylation reactions. It works with B12 in the methylation cycle and is critical for cell division and neural tube development.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Folate deficiency causes large red blood cells
Folate essential for fetal neural tube closure
Folate deficiency raises homocysteine levels

dietary_interventions.intervention: Leafy green vegetables, legumes, fortified grains

biomarker_interactions.description: B12 activates folate; deficiency of either raises homocysteine
Folate normalizes elevated homocysteine

biomarkers.name: Hemoglobin
biomarkers.physiological_role: Hemoglobin is the oxygen-carrying protein in red blood cells, composed of four heme groups each containing iron. It binds oxygen in the lungs and releases it in peripheral tissues. Hemoglobin concentration is a key indicator of blood's oxygen-carrying capacity.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low hemoglobin defines anemia
Elevated hemoglobin may indicate polycythemia or dehydration
Low hemoglobin reduces oxygen delivery to tissues

dietary_interventions.intervention: Vitamin C to enhance iron absorption

biomarker_interactions.description: Hematocrit is the volume fraction of RBCs; closely linked to HGB
Iron is essential for hemoglobin synthesis
Ferritin reflects iron stores available for HGB production

biomarkers.name: Platelets
biomarkers.physiological_role: Platelets are small blood fragments essential for hemostasis and thrombosis. They adhere to damaged blood vessels, aggregate to form clots, and release growth factors. Platelet count and function are important for cardiovascular and wound healing.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low platelets increase bleeding risk
Elevated platelets increase thrombosis risk

biomarker_interactions.description: MPV reflects platelet size and activation
Inflammation affects platelet function

biomarkers.name: Transferrin saturation
biomarkers.physiological_role: Transferrin saturation (TSAT) is the percentage of transferrin (iron- transport protein) that is saturated with iron. It indicates how much iron is available for tissues and is used with ferritin to assess iron status.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low TSAT with high TIBC indicates iron deficiency
High TSAT (>45%) suggests iron overload

biomarker_interactions.description: TSAT = (serum iron / TIBC) x 100
TIBC increases when iron is low
Both assess iron status

biomarkers.name: Total iron binding capacity
biomarkers.physiological_role: TIBC measures the maximum amount of iron that transferrin can bind. It increases when iron stores are low and decreases in iron overload, chronic disease, or malnutrition. TIBC is used with serum iron to calculate transferrin saturation.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: High TIBC with low iron indicates iron deficiency anemia
Low TIBC in anemia of chronic disease

biomarker_interactions.description: TSAT = (serum iron / TIBC) x 100
TIBC used to calculate transferrin saturation
Ferritin and TIBC provide complementary iron status info

biomarkers.name: Iron
biomarkers.physiological_role: Serum iron is the amount of circulating iron bound to transferrin. Iron is essential for oxygen transport (hemoglobin), electron transport (cytochromes), and as a cofactor for many enzymes. Iron levels fluctuate throughout the day and with inflammation.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low iron with low ferritin indicates iron deficiency
High iron may indicate hemochromatosis or iron overload

biomarker_interactions.description: Ferritin stores iron; together assess iron status
TIBC measures iron-binding capacity
TSAT = (serum iron / TIBC) x 100

biomarkers.name: Hematocrit
biomarkers.physiological_role: Hematocrit is the percentage of total blood volume that is red blood cells. It reflects the blood's oxygen-carrying capacity and is used to diagnose and monitor anemia, polycythemia, and hydration status.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low hematocrit indicates anemia
Elevated hematocrit increases blood viscosity and thrombosis risk

biomarker_interactions.description: HCT is approximately 3x HGB; closely linked
HCT reflects RBC volume fraction

biomarkers.name: Mean platelet volume
biomarkers.physiological_role: MPV measures the average size of platelets. Larger platelets are younger and more reactive. MPV is used to evaluate platelet production and destruction and is associated with inflammatory conditions and cardiovascular risk.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Elevated MPV associated with increased platelet reactivity and CV risk
MPV elevated in acute inflammation

biomarker_interactions.description: MPV and platelet count often have inverse relationship

biomarkers.name: Red blood cell count
biomarkers.physiological_role: RBC count measures the number of red blood cells per volume of blood. RBCs contain hemoglobin and transport oxygen from lungs to tissues. RBC count is part of the complete blood count and helps diagnose anemia and polycythemia.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low RBC count indicates anemia
Elevated RBC suggests polycythemia vera or secondary erythrocytosis

biomarker_interactions.description: RBC, HGB, and HCT all reflect oxygen-carrying capacity
RBC and HCT are closely correlated

biomarkers.name: Mean cell hemoglobin
biomarkers.physiological_role: MCH is the average amount of hemoglobin per red blood cell, calculated from hemoglobin and RBC count. It classifies anemia as normocytic, microcytic, or macrocytic and helps determine the cause of abnormal hemoglobin values.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: Low MCH (<27) indicates iron deficiency or thalassemia
High MCH (>33) seen in B12/folate deficiency

biomarker_interactions.description: MCH and MCHC are related; both classify RBCs
All RBC indices related

biomarkers.name: Mean cell hemoglobin concentration
biomarkers.physiological_role: MCHC is the average concentration of hemoglobin in red blood cells, expressed as grams per deciliter of RBCs. It is useful for classifying anemia and detecting abnormalities in hemoglobin concentration.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: High MCHC (>36) seen in hereditary spherocytosis
Low MCHC (<32) indicates iron deficiency

biomarker_interactions.description: MCH and MCHC related; both classify RBCs
All RBC indices related

biomarkers.name: Red cell distribution width
biomarkers.physiological_role: RDW measures the variation in red blood cell size (anisocytosis). It is elevated in conditions where RBCs are abnormally small (microcytes) or large (macrocytes), such as iron deficiency, B12/folate deficiency, or thalassemia.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: High RDW with low MCV indicates iron deficiency
High RDW with high MCV suggests megaloblastic anemia
Very high RDW may indicate multiple nutritional deficiencies

biomarker_interactions.description: RDW and MCV together help classify anemia
Both used in anemia workup

biomarkers.name: Mean corpuscular volume
biomarkers.physiological_role: MCV is the average volume of red blood cells, used to classify anemia as normocytic (normal), microcytic (small), or macrocytic (large). It helps identify the underlying cause of abnormal hemoglobin levels.

associated_health_outcomes.evidence: MCV <80 indicates iron deficiency or thalassemia
MCV >100 indicates B12/folate deficiency or liver disease

biomarker_interactions.description: MCV and MCH are correlated
RDW and MCV together classify anemia
